speaker: Guest
text: Undeclared wars are commonplace. Tragically, our government engages in preemptive war otherwise known as aggression with no complaints from the American people. Sadly, we have become accustomed to living with the illegitimate use of force by government. To develop a truly free society, the issue of initiating force must be understood and rejected.

speaker: Guest
text: What if sometimes, to love your country, you had to alter or abolish the government? What if Jefferson was right? What if that government is best which governs least? What if it is dangerous to be right when the government is wrong? What if it is better to perish fighting for freedom than to live as a slave? What if freedom's greatest hour of danger is now?

speaker: Host
text: Hi, everyone. Judge Andrew Napolitano here for Judging Freedom. Today is Tuesday, May 26, 2026. Professor John Mearsheimer will be with us in just a moment on the neocons and their lust for war. But first this.

speaker: Host
text: Professor Mearsheimer, welcome here. Before we get to a general conversation about the neocons and the dynamite, dynamite clip from the debate that you and Professor Walt had with Mrs. Newland and Secretary Pompeo, did the United States just resume attacking Iranian military assets, in this case, speedboats.

speaker: Guest
text: Yes, they did. It's really hard to figure out what's going on here. If you think about it, when we got together last Tuesday, the 19th, I thought we were going to talk about the war restarting, the air campaign beginning again,

speaker: Guest
text: because that's what Trump had promised on the 18th, last Monday. But instead, he said. On the 19th, when we got together, that peace negotiations had started again and he had been convinced not to start the bombing campaign. Then on Friday,

speaker: Guest
text: he announced that he wasn't going to his son's wedding and it looked like the bombing campaign was going to start over this past weekend, over Memorial Day weekend. But then on Saturday and Sunday, word came out that they were close to an agreement.

speaker: Guest
text: And you started saying to yourself, oh, here we go. We're going to get an agreement now. And then yesterday, Monday, we're bombing targets in Iran again. It's hard to make sense of where we're going on this roller coaster ride.

speaker: Host
text: The Iranian negotiators are apparently in Doha. trying to work out some kind of a deal there and within hours of their arrival is when the U.S. attacked these speedboats supposedly it said for self-defense. Is there a ceasefire or isn't there? Are there negotiations or aren't there? This is truly unorthodox. In fact, calling it unorthodox is being charitable. It's crazy.

speaker: Guest
text: The whole just talk about the negotiating process. First of all, it appears that on our side, we have one person and one person only who's involved in this process. And that person is President Trump,

speaker: Guest
text: who constantly is talking about this aspect of the deal or that aspect of the deal. And he's sort of making things up as he goes along. He's changing his mind from day to day. This is just no way to conduct a negotiation.

speaker: Guest
text: This is something that should be done in private. There should be professional teams on both sides instead of on just one side. And they ought to try to work out the differences so that we can shut this war down.

speaker: Guest
text: But if you listen to a lot of what President Trump says and then what the Iranians follow up with, it's quite clear that many of the points that President Trump is making, many of the points he's making are.

speaker: Guest
text: are at odds with what is the Iranian position, and there is no way they would accept Trump's various demands. It's a rather bizarre process, and it's hard to see how it leads to a meaningful deal.

speaker: Host
text: Well, also over the weekend was a massive attack on governmental buildings in Kiev. An arrest was used, although according to Alistair, the arrest was not used on those buildings so that it wouldn't kill civilians. It was used on other assets on the outskirts of Kiev. But all of this taken together is Putin taking the gloves off. What is the significance of an attack of this magnitude?

speaker: Guest
text: Well, it's quite significant because it does appear he's taking the gloves off, and the Russians have made it clear that the United States and other countries should get their diplomats out of Kiev because that was just the first of what will be a string of attacks on Kiev.

speaker: Guest
text: It looks like he's going to really pound the infrastructure in Kiev in serious ways that... have not been done before. And I think this does reflect the fact he's taking the gloves off.

speaker: Host
text: And do you expect a European response? I don't mean verbally, I mean militarily.

speaker: Guest
text: No, I think what the Europeans will do is they will try to increase the number and the quality of the drones that they give to Ukraine and go to considerable lengths to facilitate more Ukrainian attacks on Russia with those drones. I mean, I think what has in good part prompted this attack by the Russians on Kiev and the threat of more attacks on Kiev.

speaker: Guest
text: is the fact that the Ukrainians have been launching more and more drone attacks on the Russians, and the Russians are reaching a point where they believe they've just had enough and it's time to retaliate and do everything they can to put an end to this.

speaker: Host
text: So is the possession of nuclear weapons a deterrent? Because it doesn't seem to scare the Ukrainians at all.

speaker: Guest
text: No, it doesn't. I mean, the fact is that for a long time now, the Ukrainians have felt free to attack the Russian homeland with missiles and with drones. And you want to remember that in August of 2024, Ukraine actually invaded Mother Russia. This is... Quite remarkable. And that invasion was done with help from the British and from the Americans.

speaker: Host
text: Is that when they took that little landlocked? province Kalingrad is that what you're talking about or you this know was technically, he's talking about Kurtz this was the curse offensive. right and it took the Russians a while to get them out

speaker: Guest
text: It did. It took them a good couple months to push them out, which they eventually did. But the mere fact that Ukraine invaded Russian territory is quite remarkable. And the fact that they did it with assistance from the British and the Americans is even more remarkable. remarkable. As I've said on numerous occasions, we would have never thought about doing this during the Cold War.

speaker: Guest
text: And then furthermore, the Ukrainians actually attacked again with British and American help one leg of the strategic nuclear triad of the Russian inventory. They went after the bomber leg of the triad. And this was again unthinkable during the Cold War. And it just went to show

speaker: Guest
text: That the Russian nuclear deterrent wasn't really deterring Ukraine from attacking Russian assets on Russian soil to include invading Russia. And there's been tremendous pressure on Trump, not on Trump, on Putin to do something about this for a long time now. But Putin has been very reluctant to up the ante, but it appears that he's now decided that something has to be done.

speaker: Guest
text: Done. And I think the bombing of Kiev is the first step in that direction.

speaker: Host
text: Well, this is asking you to predict what might have happened, but what might have happened if Iran did have a nuclear weapon? Would the United States and Israel have attacked it as boldly and viciously as they did in June and February?

speaker: Guest
text: I think the answer is categorically no. And I think all you have to do is look at North Korea to realize just how important it is to have a nuclear weapon when you're dealing with a country like the United States or a country like Israel that might attack you.

speaker: Guest
text: The United States would not attack North Korea for fear that North Korea would launch its nuclear weapons at South Korea. South Korea and Japan and American military forces that are stationed in those two countries. So from North Korea's point of view, they have the ultimate deterrent. Iran,

speaker: Guest
text: of course, does not. And before February 28th, I don't think many people thought that Iran could shut the Strait down or would shut the Strait down. And that would be a formidable deterrent in itself. But now we know that even though Iran doesn't have nuclear weapons,

speaker: Guest
text: it can shut down the Strait. And one could argue that moving forward, the Iranians will have less of an incentive to get nuclear weapons than they otherwise would have because they can shut the Strait down. But nevertheless, if I were an Iranian policymaker, I'd eat grass if that was necessary to acquire a nuclear weapon, because once you have a nuclear weapon. weapon.

speaker: Guest
text: It is extremely unlikely that the Israelis or the Americans will attack you again for fear of ending up in a nuclear war.

speaker: Guest
text: They don't, which is, I think, quite remarkable. And I think even the Europeans has We all know there's lots of talk inside of Russia these days about attacking targets in Europe, in other words, attacking NATO states that are aiding the Ukrainian offensive against Russia.

speaker: Guest
text: Again, because the Russians are fed up and because the Europeans are supporting the Ukrainians, there are powerful incentives for the Russians to attack European states. And these are European states. European states that are in NATO and therefore have an Article 5 guarantee, which means the United States could be dragged into the war.

speaker: Guest
text: Now, most of the rhetoric that you hear coming out of Russia talks about using conventional weapons to attack into Europe, but a number of commentators inside of Russia, and these are prominent commentators, say that if the conventional weapons don't do the trick, Russia should think about using small numbers.

speaker: Guest
text: of nuclear weapons to send a clear message to the West that we live in a nuclear era or in the nuclear era and that Russia is armed to the teeth with nuclear weapons and it will use those weapons if it thinks its survival's at stake.

speaker: Host
text: Does the West fear Russia? Does Western Europe fear Russia?

speaker: Guest
text: Well, there's no question the Western elites or the European elites fear Russia. Whether the public does is another matter. I think the elites in Europe, especially in Western Europe, have convinced themselves that the Russians are coming and that something has to be done to create a deterrent to prevent that. In Eastern Europe, there's always been a fear.

speaker: Guest
text: and a fear of the Russians. So there's no need to create a boogeyman there. The boogeyman has been there from the beginning. But now in Western Europe, there is this sense that the Russians are coming and something has to be done to contain the Russians. I think this is a fallacious argument. I think the Russians are not even interested in conquering all of Ukraine,

speaker: Guest
text: much less Eastern Europe. And the idea that they're going to conquer any territory. territory in Western Europe is laughable. I don't know what... These elites are thinking about when they look at how much difficulty the Russians have had conquering the Western, excuse me, the Eastern one-fifth of Ukraine over the past four plus years.

speaker: Guest
text: This doesn't look like the Wehrmacht. This doesn't look like an army that's poised to overrun Eastern Europe, much less all of Europe. And I think it's not even going to conquer all of Ukraine. I think this is.

speaker: Guest
text: There's virtually no chance of that happening. But nevertheless, the European elites have convinced themselves that they're facing a truly serious threat from Russia, not altogether unlike the Soviet threat during the Cold War,

speaker: Guest
text: and they're now going to set out to deal with this threat. But the big problem they face is the Americans are pulling out of Europe in certain ways, and this means that NATO is no longer the formidable instrument it was.

speaker: Guest
text: It was during the Cold War. And furthermore, the manufacturing capabilities of these European countries are such that they don't have the ability to produce large numbers of weapons to contain the Russians.

speaker: Host
text: Let's talk about the neocons with whom you had an encounter in. in Canada recently when you and Professor Walt debated former Secretary of State and CIA Director Mike Pompeo and the cookie lady.

I don't know if it's true or not. What is their thing? What is their argument? What is the core of their belief which is inconsistent with reality?

speaker: Guest
text: Well, their basic worldview is that the United States should be prepared to go around the world and slay monsters. Now, you say to yourself, what do they mean by monsters? There's no clear definition of what a monster is. A monster is a country that has interests that are at odds with the United States, that might be a non-democracy that they don't like.

speaker: Guest
text: And they believe that American military force provides a magic instrument for transforming these monster states into friendly states. And they believe that if the United States... is smart, what it will do is it will try to do social engineering all across the globe. It will try to change regimes that are not friendly to the United States and the regimes that are friendly to the United States.

speaker: Guest
text: And of course, once you end up making the world a place that's filled with countries that look like the United States, we will end up living happily ever after. speaker: Guest

The Bush doctrine disappeared, although it then got resurrected.

We don't know all the details at this point, but the details we do know shows that she was a key player. And I think that there's no question that the decision to bring Ukraine into NATO.

speaker: Guest
